questions.question_text: DC imposes two separate taxes on real property transfers. Which party typically pays each?
questions.correct_answer: Seller pays transfer tax; buyer pays recordation tax
distractors: Buyer pays both taxes at closing
Seller pays both taxes at closing
Each party pays half of both taxes
questions.explanation: DC's two-tax system assigns the transfer tax to the seller and the recordation tax to the buyer. Both are paid at closing when the deed and deed of trust are recorded.

questions.question_text: A DC home sells for $350,000. What is the combined transfer and recordation tax?
questions.explanation: For properties under $400,000, the transfer tax is 1.1% and the recordation tax is 1.1%, totaling 2.2%. $350,000 x 2.2% = $7,700.

questions.question_text: At what purchase price does the higher DC transfer and recordation tax rate apply?
questions.explanation: The higher rate tier (1.45% each for transfer and recordation, totaling 2.9%) applies to residential properties selling for $400,000 or more. Below $400,000, each tax is 1.1%.

questions.question_text: A DC condo sells for $600,000. What is the combined transfer and recordation tax rate?
questions.explanation: At $600,000 (above the $400,000 threshold), the combined rate is 2.9%: 1.45% transfer tax (seller) + 1.45% recordation tax (buyer).

questions.question_text: Which transfers are exempt from DC transfer and recordation taxes?
questions.explanation: Certain transfers are exempt from DC transfer and recordation taxes, including transfers between spouses, certain trust and estate transfers, and government-to-government transfers.

questions.question_text: What benefit does DC offer qualifying first-time homebuyers at closing?
questions.correct_answer: A reduced recordation tax rate of 0.725%
distractors: Complete exemption from all transfer taxes
A 50% reduction in the transfer tax
questions.explanation: Eligible first-time DC homebuyers can reduce their recordation tax from 1.45% to 0.725%. The seller's transfer tax remains unchanged. This applies to the buyer's portion only.

questions.question_text: The DC first-time homebuyer recordation tax reduction applies to which type of tax?
distractors: Both the transfer and recordation taxes
All closing costs including title insurance
questions.explanation: The first-time homebuyer benefit reduces only the buyer's recordation tax (from 1.45% to 0.725%). The seller's transfer tax rate remains unchanged. The purchase price must fall within an annually adjusted limit.

questions.question_text: A first-time DC homebuyer purchases a $500,000 condo. What recordation tax rate applies?
questions.explanation: As a first-time homebuyer purchasing below the $777,000 limit, the reduced recordation tax rate of 0.725% applies instead of the standard 1.45% rate for properties over $400,000.

questions.question_text: To qualify for the DC first-time homebuyer recordation tax reduction, the property must be:
distractors: Located in a designated opportunity zone
An investment property with rental income
questions.explanation: The first-time homebuyer benefit requires the property to be the buyer's primary residence. The buyer must have never owned eligible property as a principal residence in DC and must meet income limits.

questions.question_text: Which of the following disqualifies a buyer from DC's first-time homebuyer tax reduction?
questions.correct_answer: Previously owning a primary residence in DC
distractors: Having rented in DC for more than 5 years
questions.explanation: A 'DC first-time homebuyer' is defined as someone who has never owned eligible property as their principal residence. Previous DC homeownership disqualifies the buyer, regardless of when it occurred.

questions.question_text: DC is considered what type of state for real estate closings?
questions.explanation: DC is an attorney state, meaning real estate closings are typically handled by settlement attorneys. A title company or settlement agent conducts the closing, with attorneys overseeing the legal aspects.

questions.question_text: Where are deeds and deeds of trust recorded in DC?
distractors: The Office of Tax and Revenue
questions.explanation: Deeds and deeds of trust in DC are recorded with the DC Recorder of Deeds. Recording provides constructive notice of ownership and lien interests.

questions.question_text: At a DC closing, which type of title insurance protects the buyer against title defects?
questions.explanation: An owner's title insurance policy protects the buyer against title defects. The lender's policy (typically required by the mortgage lender) protects only the lender's interest, not the buyer's.

questions.question_text: What does a DC settlement agent do as part of the closing process?
questions.correct_answer: Conducts a title search and prepares closing documents
distractors: Appraises the property for the buyer
Inspects the property for structural defects
Negotiates the purchase price between parties
questions.explanation: The settlement agent conducts a title search, prepares the Closing Disclosure and other closing documents, coordinates signing, and ensures all taxes and fees are properly paid and recorded.

questions.question_text: DC uses which type of security instrument for real estate financing?
questions.explanation: DC uses deeds of trust (not mortgages) as the primary security instrument. A deed of trust involves three parties: the borrower (grantor), lender (beneficiary), and trustee (neutral third party).
